data.description:
Oscillation and The Cross-Boundary Problem
The most significant risk inherent in automated convergence loops is the threat of infinite oscillation, a phenomenon most frequently triggered by the Cross-Boundary Problem.
This occurs when a defect identified in the reviewed code is actually a symptom of a root cause located in a different architectural domain. For instance, a reviewer agent analyzing a frontend component might correctly identify an error handling flaw, but the true fix requires a structural change to a backend server API. An unsophisticated prompt will cause the reviewer to flag the frontend code and demand a client-side workaround. The autonomous fixer applies the workaround, but upon re-review, the reviewer agent flags the workaround as a negative architectural "code smell," demanding it be removed. This initiates an endless, costly cycle of applying and reverting the exact same mitigation. 
To resolve this, advanced prompt schemas force the reviewer model to output a bo[span_100](start_span)[span_100](end_span)undary_owner field alongside its finding. By evaluating the architectural boundaries, the LLM determines which service should genuinely own the fix. If the boundary_owner does not match the currently reviewed service, the finding is formally logged for human architectural review but is explicitly excluded from the automated convergence check, cleanly severing the oscillation loop and allowing the PR to proceed.
data.title: Oscillation and The Cross-Boundary Problem
